dialogues.name: TIM I

dialogues.text: guess we have to do the Lake House now on Dune Pod. Alright. So this heist scene is just great. The fights are awesome. The way they move is really weird.

dialogues.text: Just to fuel the engines. So but then also, it's a boat, right and as beautiful like you know these beautiful wood decks, and like they've got to bring all this equipment on. I read some article about how they basically had like a crew of like 10 people that all they were doing was just -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Holding stuff?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Asset protection. Like just to make sure that they like didn't scuff the floors and you know, didn't fuck shit up.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: That's what, the movie we did last week or two weeks ago, Lost Highway, David Lynch ended up buying the house because he wanted to shoot this house and he didn't want to shoot at home. And the way that they did that was they brought that - and they knew they couldn't do that because that was gonna really fuck up the boat, so they brought the helicopter down within like three feet of the deck and like just like cut it to make it look like they landed the helicopter but never actually ended the helicopter on the boat. I mean, it had to be like 10s of millions of dollars just to do the boat stuff, like before you get into any other part of the movie, which is quite extensive.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: This is where people forget like this is where being a producer sucks because you're sitting there looking at this billion dollar you know, multi million dollar asset and you're like, just don't...just don't scratch it. Come on, please don't scratch it guys!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Please don't fuck up the boat. Oh my god, I'd be so stressed. I'd be so stressed.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: $205 million for the budget for Tenet, so.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: $205 million?

dialogues.name: JASON I
dialogues.text: mean, at least 10% went to boat related costs. [Tim laughs]

dialogues.name: TIM I
dialogues.text: mean for sure. There's ways to shoot around it. I mean, they could have they definitely built some of the interior. Yeah, all the interiors are staged.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Oh for sure!

dialogues.text: You could even cheat the back sundeck on like, some different kind of setup. But it looked expensive.

dialogues.text: [in Sator voice] Going to watch a man choke on his testicles... or whatever it was. Like, this is, this is intense. [Haitch laughs]

But what happens is, Sator comes in from the end, and goes back in time.

dialogues.text: Yeah, he's doing his own pincer.

dialogues.name: JASON A
dialogues.text: temporal pincer.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah. And so you see that sort of start to come in the middle of the heist where cars are driving backwards on their move, which I was thinking about it today, they're driving forwards against the forward movers who are moving backwards in time based on the direction they're going. So that's why they're driving in the way that they're driving. Which is super cool.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: It was super cool. And again, it's another moment where you just have to watch it, don't think about it. It doesn't make a ton of sense, but just enjoy this moment.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah/

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: It's incredible.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah. How does the car keep driving? [Tim laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Which one? After the -

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: After everyone, after they abandoned ship and leave Kat by herself?

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: think they had autopilot, 80 mile an hour reverse autopilot. [Tim laughs]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Fair, fair. I was, I didn't look at the model I wanted to see maybe this is a self driving SUV. Every everything else -

dialogues.text: Well couldn't it just be on cruise control?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, no, the car is, that car is probably reversed.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah, but, do you think? This is my analytical brain, I like Jason said I'd love to get into things and figure him out. So it's really hard for me to enjoy things.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Right.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Like right, overall, it's hard for me to enjoy movies because I watch them and I think, okay, what was this shot at? What's that? I think about the technical aspects of it. But I really, really tried to think more about the artistic aspects when watching this movie, because I knew I was doing this. But that particular moment, that's the only thing I think out of all the the temporal radiation moments where I was like that, I don't understand why a car still going? There's nobody in it. Nobody's driving it. It didn't stop.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Fair enough.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: But it did lead for a dramatic moment where he -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: He gets to jump into the car and jump into the car to hit to hit the brakes. So from there, he gets captured. And so they go back, you know, Sator's men take him back. And so they're tortured on two sides of the, of the turnstiles. You got two rooms look at each other with glass in between.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: One side blue, one side red.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Exactly. And so Sator, you know, is kind of shooting on one side, shoots Kat, which we see and he's threatening her and then another Sator comes into the room and then goes through the turnstile and goes out to the other the other room and is able to disappear. Basically going back into the past and leaving Prot to have to follow him back into the past as well as to take Kat and Neil send them in the past. So that cat doesn't die because she's shot.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah. And now he's armed with all the information of the past right to affect it.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: They both are!

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yes, exactly, yeah.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So that scene is just amazing, the first time - and it was, what I remember from the trailer when I saw it about six months before the movie came out, the shot with him where he has the mask, you have to be breathing, like the only thing that doesn't work is you can't breathe right when you're going reverse against time. But the shot when like the door opens and it's like just him full frame his face, just, amazing!

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Something he did to his body. I don't know what it was, but he had just a look of malaise, like illness like -

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, yeah, that's well spotted.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: That was really well performed as far as communicating that, because he obviously doesn't say anything because by himself.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: And then the birds are flying backwards. Right, the smoke is going backwards. So you you see it, he steps into a puddle, but like the waters already dripping. Like that was so freakin cool. Gosh, I love that.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Mhmm.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: In the end, he's able to, you know, he gets the device. It turns out that they didn't show it to us, but he had thrown the device to himself. So that's a nice little reveal that he's got the device but then Sator catches him and Wrexham and is able to light his car on fire and get away with a device. And so the Protagonist is found later by Neil.

dialogues.name: TIM I
dialogues.text: think we got to talk about the device, we haven't really explained device, like -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Describe the device to us.

dialogues.name: TIM I
dialogues.text: mean, you can kind of see it at the end when it's fully assembled. But the whole idea of a math equation made into a physical form, which made me think instantly of the Mars Rover, and how they put that code in the parachute.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Oh, yeah.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: There's gotta be some sort of, like, correlation there with how many sided things or -

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Oh, yeah.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Or the distance between them. So I'm curious if they ever did an actual like -

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: What is this?

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah, exactly. That's just the nerdy part of me. That was like, I want to get into that.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Now I'm curious about that.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah. Right?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: That's great.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: And so yes, it's a doomsday device. To save their world.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Well, one question I have is why like, if you are the scientist who then created it and buried it, and then and then killed yourself, like, couldn't you have just like, not made it? [Tim laughs] Like don't write it down?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah.

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: know there's no movie without it.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah, no, exactly. Like we didn't - that's not the movie we're watching. Credits! [Tim & Haitch laugh] Very sad movie.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Can we talk about the grandfather paradox here, then? [clip from Tenet plays]

dialogues.name: THE PROTAGONIST
dialogues.text: The what?

dialogues.name: NEIL
dialogues.text: If you traveled back in time and killed your grandfather, how can you have been born so that you can commit the act?

dialogues.name: THE PROTAGONIST
dialogues.text: What's the answer?

dialogues.name: NEIL
dialogues.text: There's no answer. It's a paradox. But in the future, those in power clearly believe that one can kick grandfather down the stairs, gouge his eyes out at, slit his throat. Without consequences.

dialogues.name: THE PROTAGONIST
dialogues.text: Can they be right?

dialogues.name: NEIL
dialogues.text: It doesn't matter. They believe it. [clip from Tenet ends]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: So like I mean the theory is is that they come across this thing and then like there's some like, some future people want to just destroy everything because it didn't work out. Like they're mad at the past they want to destroy everyone.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yeah we've ruined their world and they want to remove us to save the world and bring back -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Something?

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Barnhouse. [Tim laughs]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: So but how - I mean so then -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So then what?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: You either believe that you can change the past to affect the future or you don't. Like that's like sort of like a binary, kind of, like theory in time travel.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: No future, but what we make.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah and but -

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: That's from Terminator. [Tim chuckles]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, but that's, but that is the difference right? Like Terminator 1 is a closed loop where nothing new happens.. Reese comes back to the past to get her pregnant so that she can have John Connor so that they can save things.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: He didn't just do it to get her pregnant, they fell in love, okay? [Tim laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, no, obviously they fell in love. My point is that it is a closed loop, it's not that history was changed -

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Yes.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: History was fulfilled. Versus in Terminator 2, it's about saying, no we're gonna go back and undo things, no fate but what we make, and we can actually change it so that Judgment Day doesn't occur.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Right.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: So [Tim sighs] yes, exactly.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: At least in the director's cut, Judgment Day does not occur. [Tim laughs]

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: So, exactly. If they kill us, like you say -

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: What happens to them? Like I mean, like how can they how can they exist? I mean because they live in the world that we made. So how can that - I mean it is the grandfather paradox. I just don't understand like how the movie, the movie kind of winks at that and just says like 'yeah whatever'.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So my preferred concept of how time travel works is that everything is either completely consistent in a closed loop like that or if you're going to allow you know a multi -

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Multi-universes.

dialogues.name: HAITCH A
dialogues.text: multi-universe way, then I think every time you go back, if you weren't there on the previous time, then you're branched. Exactly. And it branches from there. So the people who are already on history that's already occurred, if they blow up the previous history it's not going to change. And actually the Protagonist says that. [clip from Tenet plays]

dialogues.name: THE PROTAGONIST
dialogues.text: That time is reversed... Does that mean we are here now, not that it never happens? That we stop them?

dialogues.name: NEIL
dialogues.text: Optimistically, I'd say that's right.

dialogues.name: THE PROTAGONIST
dialogues.text: And pessimistically?

dialogues.name: NEIL
dialogues.text: In a parallel worlds theory, we can know the relationship between... consciousness and multiple realities. Does you head hurt yet?

dialogues.name: THE PROTAGONIST
dialogues.text: Yes. [clip from Tenet ends]

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Where we see - yeah, like that - have either of you read Peripheral by William Gibson?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yes!

dialogues.name: TIM I
dialogues.text: think this movie and that series, which is two books now, Peripheral and -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Agency.

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: Agency, yes.

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: have not read it. Any good? The second one?

dialogues.name: TIM I
dialogues.text: liked Peripheral a lot better. Agency I thought was fun and it's a continuation of the story so I'm excited for that. Basically if you have a strong enough computer in this world you can, and in a broadband, you can reach back in time to touch a nearby generation and create a stub or divergence within the timeline to try and change the past.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: You can transmit data, you can't transmit anything physical but you can transmit information.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Right, just data. So that sounds pretty cool, that's great. So that's what, when I watched this movie, that's what I initially thought of and I kind of still think that in Peripheral it's handled maybe a little bit gracefully, or a little bit better. Because again you're not, you can't affect your timeline but you can give these people a better chance to have a different outcome and so that's I guess maybe what he's villains want to do?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Alright, I mean I think some of this also just exists for dramatic tension, right?

dialogues.name: TIM
dialogues.text: of course.

dialogues.name: JASON
